subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Polar Curves & Area
stimulus: Let $S$ be the region bounded by the graph of the polar curve $r(\theta) = 3\theta\sin(\theta^2)$ for $0 \leq \theta \leq \sqrt{\pi}$, as shown in the figure above.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral setup for polar area: $\frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\sqrt{\pi}} [r(\theta)]^2\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\sqrt{\pi}} [3\theta\sin(\theta^2)]^2\,d\theta$
Correct numerical answer: area $\approx 4.382$ (or $\frac{9\pi}{4} - \frac{9}{4} \approx 4.382$, accept any equivalent value)

parts.prompt: What is the average distance from the origin to a point on the polar curve $r(\theta) = 3\theta\sin(\theta^2)$ for $0 \leq \theta \leq \sqrt{\pi}$?

scoring_points.description: Correct numerical answer: average distance $\approx 1.076$ (or $\frac{6}{\sqrt{\pi}} \approx 3.385 / \sqrt{\pi}$, accept equivalent)

parts.prompt: There is a line $y = mx$ through the origin with positive slope $m$ that divides the region $S$ into two regions with equal areas. Write, but do not solve, an equation involving one or more integrals whose solution gives the value of $m$.

scoring_points.description: Recognizes that the line $y = mx$ corresponds to the ray $\theta = \arctan(m)$, so the dividing angle is $\theta_0 = \arctan(m)$
Correct polar area integral for one sub-region: $\frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\arctan(m)} [3\theta\sin(\theta^2)]^2\,d\theta$

Correct equation setting the area of the lower sub-region equal to half the total area: $\frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\arctan(m)} 9\theta^2\sin^2(\theta^2)\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\sqrt{\pi}} 9\theta^2\sin^2(\theta^2)\,d\theta$

parts.prompt: For $k > 0$, let $A(k)$ be the area of the portion of region $S$ that is also inside the circle $r = k\cos\theta$. Find $\lim_{k \to \infty} A(k)$.

scoring_points.description: Correct reasoning: as $k \to \infty$, the circle $r = k\cos\theta$ grows without bound and eventually contains all of region $S$, so $A(k)$ approaches the total area of $S$

required_elements: As $k \to \infty$, the circle $r = k\cos\theta$ contains all of $S$; therefore $A(k) \to$ area of $S$
